title: Game Components
summary: Describes the physical and informational components included with each GOSS game. References the two game rules booklets and all supporting materials.
text: **1.0 GAME COMPONENTS**

title: Game Rules
summary: There are two game rules booklets: the GOSS rules common to all games in the series and the scenario rules specific to each game. Rules in a numbered case format give precedence to higher numbered rules within the same section.
text:
There are two game rules booklets. The **GOSS** rules are common to all games in the series; consider these the basic rules set. The scenario rules are specific to each game. The Scenario rules cover all special rules, scenarios, and setups that pertain to that game.

The rules are in a numbered case format, with higher numbered rules within the same section always taking precedence over lowered numbered rules. Rules in the scenario booklet may change, add to, and always supersede any rules found in the basic rules set.

title: Game Map
summary: Each game map is a graphic representation of the geographic area of the game's battle, with a hex grid regulating distance and location. Terrain features exist both within hexes and along hexsides, and the defender always chooses the terrain feature in the defending hex.
text:
Each game map in the series is a graphic representation of the geographic area in which a game's battle occurred. The map's hex grid regulates distance and location for various game functions. Each hex has a unique identifying number printed within it. Terrain features exist both within hexes and along hexsides; the Terrain Key lists these features.

**Important:** The defender always chooses the terrain feature in the defending hex. When moving a unit, always use the most expensive terrain cost for both hexes and hexsides, unless using a form of road movement.

title: Game Charts & Tables
summary: Each game comes with various charts and displays. See the Definitions, Abbreviations & Acronyms section for information on the various charts, tables, and tracks.
text: Each game in the series comes with various charts and displays. See Definitions, Abbreviations & Acronyms section at the end of the rules for information concerning the various charts, tables and tracks.

title: The Playing Pieces
summary: Each game includes cardboard counters: unit counters representing HQs and combat units, and marker counters recording information on the game map or off-map record tracks.
text:
Included with each game are cardboard pieces (counters).

• Unit counters (units), represent headquarters (HQs) and combat units that took part in the battle. Players can find detailed counter information on the UTC.

• All other counters (markers) record or keep track of information on the game map or on off-map record tracks. Markers also keep track of any change in a unit's supply state, strength, or mode.

**Important:** When calculating the GA or FS strength of units:
**1)** Apply modifiers to each unit individually (do not round each individual unit).
**2)** Total the modified strength of all participating units.
**3)** Round the total final value up to the next whole number.

**Example:** A stack of 2 Inf Bn.'s and 1 Eng Co. are defending against a ground assault. The stack is out of supply. Halve each unit's combat factors. Each Bn. has a printed defense factor of 5 and the Eng Co. has a printed defense factor of 3. The total would be (5÷2=2.5) + (5÷2=2.5) + (3÷2=1.5) = 6.5 rounded up to 7.

title: Punching Out the Counters
summary: Players should make photocopies of each counter sheet prior to punching out the counters so they can more easily identify units during play.
text: Players should make photocopies of each counter sheet prior to punching out the counters. It will be much easier to find a counter if you have a copy of the counter sheets handy to quickly ID the unit.

title: The Rounding Rule
summary: Unless a rule states otherwise, always round fractional numbers up to the next whole number. A rule may also require rounding to the nearest whole number using the standard mathematical rounding rule.
text:
Unless a rule states otherwise, always round fractional numbers up to the next whole number.
**Example:** Round 1.3 up to 2.
**Exceptions:** Formation Value (15.4.2a).

A rule may require the player to round fractions to the nearest whole number using the standard mathematical rounding rule.
**Example:** Round 1.3 down to 1. Round 1.5 up to 2.

title: Game Scale
summary: Each hex is one mile of actual distance from side to side. Units represent Co.'s and Bn.'s, with each AM or PM GT equaling about one-half of available daylight time.
text: Each hex is one mile of actual distance from side to side. Units represent Co.'s and Bn.'s. Each AM or PM GT equals about one-half of available daylight time. The night GT represents the hours of darkness. ENA game turns represent additional effort on the part of troops at night when they normally would be sleeping or refitting.

title: Inventory of Game Parts
summary: Each game provides a complete list of game components.
text: Each game provides a complete list of game components.

title: Setting Up the Game
summary: Covers terminology for formations and their relationship to the hierarchy of command, which is an important function of the game. Players should familiarize themselves with the following terms: regiments, brigades, divisions, battle groups and formations (9.0 & 26.0).
text: **Important:** The terminology used to define formations and their relationship to the hierarchy of command is an important function of the game. Players should familiarize themselves with the following terms; regiments, brigades, divisions, battle groups and formations (9.0 & 26.0).

title: Choosing Sides
summary: Players choose which side they will play. The Allied side controls all Allied units and the Axis side controls all German, Italian, and other Axis allied units. National force composition for each game is in the scenario rules.
text:
The players choose which side they will play.

• The Allied side controls all Allied units including all American (US), British/Commonwealth (CW), and any other minor Allied units (such as Free French).

• The Axis side controls all German (including *SS*, *Kriegsmarine*, and *Luftwaffe*) Italian and other minor Axis allied units.

*Note: National force composition for each game are in the scenario rules.*

title: Setting up the Map & Pieces
summary: If a game has more than one map sheet, the scenario rules explain how the maps lay out. Players sort units and markers by formations and usage, set them up on the map according to the scenario rules, and can identify hexes by map identifier and hex number.
text:
If a game consists of more than one map sheet, the scenario rules will explain how the maps lay out. Players should sort units and markers by formations and usage.

• Once players choose a scenario, units that begin the scenario on the map are set up according to the scenario rules.

• When setting up units, players can identify hexes by the map identifier (letter or number) and the hex number printed in each hex.

title: The Game Turn
summary: The Sequence of Play (SoP) divides each GT into phases, which may be sub-divided into segments. Each GD of historical time is composed of three GTs: AM, PM, and night turn, and players may prolong a Night GT using extended night activity (ENA) if either side desires.
text:
The Sequence of Play (SoP) divides each GT into phases.

• Phases may be further sub-divided into smaller periods of game activity called segments.

• Each GD of historical time is composed of three GTs: AM, PM, and night turn. Players may prolong a Night GT using extended night activity (ENA) if either side desires it.

title: Scenario First Turn Rules
summary: Scenarios may begin with a GT that has a non-standard SoP. Scenario rules will explain when and how this occurs.
text: Scenarios may begin with a GT that has a non-standard SoP. Scenario rules will explain when and how this occurs.

title: Game Turn Record Track
summary: The GTRT can be located on the map or on a stand-alone chart and may contain historical date and GD number, historical weather, available air/truck points, reinforcement lists, unit withdrawals, and Replacement Tables. Players use GD and GT markers to record the passage of days and GTs.
text:
The GTRT can be located on the map or on a stand-alone chart. The GTRT may contain the following information:

**a)** Historical date and GD number.
**b)** Historical weather.
**c)** Number of available air/truck points.
**d)** Reinforcement lists.
**e)** Unit withdrawals.
**f)** Replacement Tables.

• In addition to the GD boxes, there are the AM, PM, and night GT Boxes. These boxes track the GTs in each GD.

• Players should place GD and GT markers in the appropriate boxes to record the passage of days and GTs.

title: Sequence of Play
summary: A GT consists of one to five preliminary joint phases, an Allied Player Turn, and an Axis Player Turn. During each Player Turn, one side is the active player and the other is the inactive player.
text:
A GT consists of:

**a)** One to five preliminary joint phases.
**b)** Allied Player Turn.
**c)** Axis Player Turn.

title: Active Player
summary: During each Player Turn, one side is the active player and the other is the inactive player. The active player performs tasks during his Player Turn, although there are exceptions as described in the SoP.
text:
During each Player Turn, one side is the active player; the other side is the inactive player.

**Example:** The Allied player is the active player during the Allied Player Turn.

• The active player performs tasks during his Player Turn, although there are exceptions, as described in the SoP.

title: Game Turn Sequence Outline
summary: Players conduct each GT in the order given below. All phases and segments must occur in the order given.
text: Players conduct each GT in the order given below. All phases and segments must occur in the order given.

title: Joint Player Pre-Game Day Activities
summary: Both sides conduct the below phases prior to the AM Allied Player Turn. The Joint Weather Phase (3.3.1b) and the Joint Fuel Value Determination Phase (3.3.1e) are also performed before every GT.
text:
Both sides conduct the below phases prior to the AM Allied Player Turn.

**Important:** The Joint Weather Phase (3.3.1b) and the Joint Fuel Value Determination Phase (3.3.1e) are also performed before every GT.

title: Joint Air Allocation Phase
summary: Scenario rules determine the number of Air Points (AP) available and any operational restrictions. Both sides determine APs for the entire GD, allocate APs to specific missions, return withdrawn Flak units to the map, and may withdraw Flak units to protect against supply interdiction attacks.
text:
Scenario rules determine the number of AP available and any operational restrictions. See 20.0 for the use of AP.

**1)** Both sides determine the number of APs they have available for the entire GD (20.1.0 & scenario rules).
**2)** Both sides allocate available AP to specific missions (20.1.1 & scenario rules).
**3)** Both sides can return withdrawn Flak units (withdrawn to protect against supply interdiction) to the map (20.7.2).
**4)** Both sides may withdraw Flak units to protect against supply interdiction attacks (20.7.2).

title: Joint Weather Phase
summary: Players conduct the Joint Weather Phase prior to the Allied Player Turn each GT (AM, PM, Night). Both sides determine the atmospheric condition, ground condition, and precipitation for the current GT and scenario rules. Both players also determine ground interdiction values.
text:
**Important:** Players conduct the Joint Weather Phase prior to the Allied Player Turn each GT (AM, PM, Night).

**Weather Segment:** Determine the atmospheric condition, ground condition, and precipitation for the current GT and scenario rules (19.0).

**Ground Interdiction Value Segment:** Both players determine ground interdiction values.

title: Joint Command Phase
summary: Both sides secretly designate which GT will be their rest GT (3.5.0). The Allied player normally must conduct rest GTs at night (3.5.1). Both sides may also attach/detach units/formations, create/alter corps and army boundaries, enter/release eligible formations to/from refit, enter/release eligible formations to/from army reserve, determine available naval units, make lull declarations, attempt to activate Ldrs, and determine if isolated units surrender.
text:
**Rest GT Designation Segment:** Both sides secretly designate which GT will be their rest GT (3.5.0).

**Important:** The Allied player normally must conduct rest GTs at night (3.5.1).

**Command Segment:** Both sides may:
**1)** Attach and/or detach units/formations (9.6.0).
**2)** Create and/or alter corps and army boundaries (9.2.0).
**3)** Enter/release eligible formations to/from refit (22.4.0).
**4)** Enter/release eligible formations to/from army reserve (25.0).

**Naval Unit Assignment Segment:** Players determine the number of available naval units (11.9.0).

**Lull Determination Segment:** Both sides may make lull declarations (24.1.0).

**Ldr Activation Segment:** Both sides attempt to activate Ldrs (23.1.0).

**Surrender Segment:** Both sides determine if isolated units surrender (15.7.1).

title: Joint Logistics Phase
summary: Both sides perform the following segments with each of their armies. Both sides should determine if there are any modifications to this phase by consulting the scenario rules.
text:
Both sides perform the following segments with each of their armies.

**Important:** Both sides should determine if there are any modifications to this phase by consulting the scenario rules.

**Truck Point Assignment Segment:**
**1)** Determine the total number of TP available to each army (16.1.0).
**2)** Allocate TP to one of the three tasks (16.1.2): ammunition, fuel, and/or motorization.

**Supply Interdiction Segment:** Both sides may conduct supply interdiction attacks (20.4.0).

**Ammo Delivery Segment:** Both sides determine the ADV for each army and corps by following the procedures in 16.2.0 & 16.3.0. ADV determined in this phase applies to all GTs in the current GD.

**Fuel Delivery Segment:**
**1)** Both sides determine the number of FPs delivered to each army (16.4.0).
**2)** Both sides allocate FP to HQs as desired (16.4.2).

**Depot Placement Segment:** Move or place new depots (15.8.0).

**Replacement Point Segment:**
**1)** Both sides convert Inf and Arm recycle steps to Inf and Arm ReP (22.1.1).
**2)** Both sides determine the type and number of RePs received for the current GD (22.3.0).

title: Joint Fuel Value Determination Phase
summary: Players conduct the Joint Fuel Value Determination Phase prior to the Allied player turn each GT (AM, PM, Night). Both sides determine the fuel value for all HQ with low or no fuel.
text:
**Important:** Players conduct the Joint Fuel Value Determination Phase prior to the Allied player turn each GT (AM, PM, Night).

Both sides determine the fuel value for all HQ with low or no fuel (16.4.5).

title: Allied Player Turn
summary: The Allied side is the active player and the Axis side is the inactive player. Unless specifically stated, the active player conducts all phases during each individual Player Turn.
text:
The Allied side is the active player; the Axis side is the inactive player.

**Important:** Unless specifically stated, the active player conducts all phases during each individual Player Turn.

title: Mode Determination Phase
summary: Determine the mode each unit will be in for this GT (5.0). This includes placing Art units in-battery or out-of-battery (5.6.0).
text: Determine the mode each unit will be in for this GT (5.0). This includes placing Art units in-battery or out-of-battery (5.6.0).

title: Construction Phase
summary: The active player conducts construction of FW and construction/repair of bridges (17.3.0), including flipping completed FW markers, advancing bridge construction, starting new construction, and demolishing bridges.
text:
**Construction Segment:** Conduct construction of FW and construction/repair of bridges (17.3.0).

**Step 1:** Flip all completed FW and bridge under-construction markers to their completed sides (17.3.0).
**Step 2:** Advance the construction of bridges by removing hit markers from under construction markers.
**Step 3:** Start construction by placing FW and bridge under-construction markers in eligible hexes.

**Important:** If time of construction is greater than one GT, place hit markers equal to the number of GTs required to complete the construction.

**Demolition Segment:** Eng units may attempt to destroy bridges (17.3.2c).

title: Movement Phase
summary: The active player conducts movement including placing Combat Reserve markers, moving units through steps 1-5, and managing reinforcements, withdrawals, command changes, strategic movement, Coup de Main attacks, and Tac mode unit movement.
text:
**Combat Reserve Designation Segment:** Place CR markers on eligible units, as desired (5.8.0).

**Movement Segment:** The active player conducts steps 1 through 5 in the order given below:

**Important:** Units in MR mode (5.4.0), Exploit mode (5.3.0) and in CR (5.8.0) may not move.

**Step 1:** The active player may:
**a)** Place reinforcements (21.0).
**b)** Conduct required withdrawals (21.4.0).
**c)** Conduct emergency command changes (9.4.1).

**Important:** The active player conducts enemy ground interdiction attacks during Step 2 through 4 against his own moving units if required (20.3.0).

**Step 2:** Units in Strat mode may move. Units moving in this segment must use Mech road movement (5.5.0).

**Step 3:** The active player declares and conducts Coup de Main (CdM) attacks (7.14.0).

**Step 4:** The active player moves units in Tac mode (5.1.0). Move units in the order given below:
**1)** Mech class units using Mech road movement (7.10.0).
**2)** All units using non-road movement (7.11.0).
During step 4, players may:
**a)** Breakdown (18.4.0 and recombine (18.7.0) units.
**b)** Mount and dismount units (7.12.0).
**c)** Place delay markers (7.13.0).

**Step 5:** Units in PA mode may move (5.2.0).

**Command Status Segment:** Determine the command status of all friendly units and HQs (9.0).

**Quick Construction Segment:** Complete eligible FW construction (17.3.3).

title: Inactive Player Exploit Phase
summary: Eligible formations in MR mode may enter Exploit mode. The inactive player declares CdM attacks. Units in Exploit may move and conduct overruns, and may be subject to ground support (GS) attacks by the active player and Art fire support (FS) missions when conducting an overrun.
text:
**Step 1:** Eligible formations in MR mode may enter Exploit mode (5.3.0).

**Step 2:** The inactive player declares CdM attacks (7.14.0).

**Step 3:** Units in Exploit may move (5.3.4) and conduct overruns (5.3.4b). Units moving during the Exploit Phase may be subject to ground support (GS) attacks (5.3.4c) by the active player while moving and may be subject to Art fire support (FS) missions when conducting an overrun (5.3.5).

**Important:** Exploit units must move in the same order as Tac mode units during the Movement Phase 7.5.2.

**AM or PM GT:** May move up to one-half of their MA in each friendly Exploit Phase during the current GT.

**Night GT and ENA Period:** The inactive player must choose to move in either the Inactive Exploit Phase or Active Exploit Phase. During the selected phase units may move their full MA (5.3.4).

title: Combat Phase
summary: The active player designates units for tactical assaults, attempts to remove delay markers, and eligible units may receive RePs. Both sides perform Fire Support (FS) missions including offensive air support, defensive support, and attacker status adjustment.
text:
**Tactical Assault Designation Segment:**
**1)** Designate units that will make tactical assaults (5.1.2 & 10.2.0).
**2)** Attempt to remove delay markers (7.13.3).

**Replacement Segment:** Eligible units may receive RePs (22.5.0).

**Fire Support (FS) Segment:** Both sides perform FS missions in the order given below.

**Offensive Air Support:** Active player conducts ground support (GS) missions (20.2.0).

**Defensive Support:** Inactive player conducts FS missions; Artillery first (11.0), followed by either GS (20.2.0) and/or Naval support as desired (11.0).

**Offensive Support:** Active player conducts FS missions; Artillery first, followed by Naval support (11.0).

**Attacker Status Adjustment Segment:**
**1)** Remove attack designation markers (TA and PA) from units that are no longer adjacent to enemy units. **2)** Remove excess attack designation markers (TA and/or PA) that are not in compliance with ADV limits (12.1.0). **3)** Voluntarily attempt to remove attack designation markers from units that are adjacent to enemy units (12.2.0).

**Ground Assault (GA) Segment:** Active player conducts GAs against enemy units (13.0). Each declared GA may require players to check for:
**a)** Hasty bridge demolition (17.3.2b).
**b)** Bridge collapse (17.3.2a).
**c)** Surrender of isolated defending units (15.7.1).

**Artillery Shift Marker Removal Segment:** Both players remove all AS markers from the map (13.13.0).

title: Active Player Exploit Phase
summary: The active player conducts this phase using the procedures in 3.3.2d.
text: The active player conducts this phase using the procedures in 3.3.2d.

title: Administrative Phase
summary: The active player conducts air resupply missions (AM & PM GTs only), checks the GenS state of all HQs and units, replenishes AD Art units, and removes one fatigue level from all eligible units. Both players may remove delay markers.
text:
**Air Resupply Segment** (AM & PM GTs only): Active player conducts air supply missions (20.5.0).

**Supply Determination Segment:** Active player checks the GenS state of all HQs and then all units marking HQ/units OhS, OoS and isolated as required (15.0).

**Ammo Replenishment Segment:**
**Step 1:** The active player may replenish AD Art units by expending AmP (16.3.7a).
**Step 2:** The active player may roll to replenish AD Art units (16.3.7b).
**Step 3:** Inactive player may replenish AD Art units by expending AmP (16.3.7a).

**Fatigue Recovery Segment:** Active player may remove one fatigue level from all eligible units (14.3.0).

**Delay Marker Removal Segment:** The owning player may remove any delay markers (player's choice). The non-owning player may remove enemy delay markers if friendly units occupy the delay marker hex (7.13.0).

title: The Axis Player Turn
summary: The Axis Player Turn is identical to the Allied Player Turn, except the Axis player is the active player and the Allied player is the inactive player. After conducting the Axis Player Turn, the GT is complete.
text: The Axis Player Turn is identical to the Allied Player Turn, except the Axis player is the active player and the Allied player is the inactive player. After conducting the Axis Player Turn, the GT is complete.

title: Update Game Turn Indicator
summary: If the last turn in the scenario is complete or if one player meets a sudden death victory condition, the game is over. Otherwise, advance the GT marker to the next GT and start a new GT.
text: If the last turn in the scenario is complete or if one player meets a sudden death victory condition, the game is over. Players should consult the scenario victory conditions to determine which side won and the level of victory obtained. If none of the above apply, advance the GT marker to the next GT and start a new GT.

title: Night GT Exploitation Phase
summary: The SoP for night GTs is identical to AM and PM GTs, with the exception that during night GTs and ENA periods, each side may only conduct Exploit activity in only one of the two allotted friendly player Exploit Phases.
text:
The SoP for night GTs is identical to AM and PM GTs.

**Exception:** During night GTs and ENA periods, each side may only conduct Exploit activity in only one of the two allotted friendly player Exploit Phases. Either during the other player's GT (during the friendly Exploit Phase) or during your GT (during the friendly Exploit Phase), but not both (5.3.4).

**Example:** It is a night GT. The German player has a formation in Exploit mode, he has two options:
**a)** During the Allied Player Turn in the Inactive Player Exploit Phase, the German player may move all units in Exploit mode up to their full MA; or,
**b)** During the German Player Turn, in the Active Player Exploit Phase, the German player may move all units in Exploit mode up to their full MA.

**Important:** The German player may not conduct Exploit movement in both.

title: Extended Night Activity
summary: At the end of the Axis Player Turn of each night GT, either side may extend the Night GT with an ENA period. This ENA period uses the special SoP outlined in 3.4.5. Units that move or conduct combat during ENA may suffer fatigue (3.4.6).
text:
At the end of the Axis Player Turn of each night GT, either side may extend the Night GT with an ENA period. This ENA period uses the special SoP outlined in 3.4.5. Units that move or conduct combat may suffer fatigue (3.4.6).

**Important:** Not all scenarios allow ENA periods.

title: Player Determination
summary: If only one side decides to conduct an ENA period, that side is automatically the first player. If both sides decide to conduct an ENA period, both sides must make ENA die rolls (1d10) to determine who is the first player and the number of unit activations.
text:
If only one side decides to conduct an ENA period, that side is automatically the first player. If both sides decide to conduct an ENA period, both sides must make ENA die rolls (1d10). The side with the higher DR (Axis wins ties) will choose which side will be the first player in the ENA SoP (3.4.5). These DRs also determine the number of unit activations for this ENA (3.4.2).

*Note: Scenario rules may award modifiers to the ENA DR.*

title: ENA Activation of Units
summary: The ENA DR result for each side is the number of units that side may attempt to activate for that ENA period. A unit cannot be OoS and/or OOC and must be in tactical or Exploit mode.
text: The ENA DR result for each side is the number of units that side may attempt to activate for that ENA period. A unit cannot be OoS and/or OOC and must be in tactical or Exploit mode.

title: ENA Activation by Leader
summary: A player may choose to use a formation leader (Ldr) to activate units. The Ldr must be active, stacked with the formation HQ or a unit of that regiment, and makes an activation check. A successful check activates all units of the formation; failure means no units of the formation may activate, but the attempt counts as one-half of the allowable number of activated units.
text:
A player may choose to use a formation leader (Ldr) to activate units.

• Ldr must be active and stacked with (in the case of formation Ldr), the formation HQ. In the case of a Rgt Ldr, the Ldr must be stacked with a unit of that Rgt.

• The Ldr makes an activation check (use Ldr's activation number). If the DR is less than this number, all units of his formation (including any attached units) may activate for ENA.

• The Ldr's attempt uses one-half of the allowable ENA activation capacity for that ENA period.

• If the Ldr failed his ENA activation DR, none of the units may activate, however, the attempt still counts as one-half of the allowable number of activated units.

**Example:** The Allied player rolled an 8 on his activation DR. General Harmon (2nd US Armored Division) is active, his activation number is 7 and he rolls a 6. All units of the 2nd Armored Division may activate for ENA, leaving the Allied player 4 units able to activate for ENA (one-half of activation DR of 8).

title: ENA Activation by Unit
summary: A player may attempt to activate each unit for ENA individually by conducting a proficiency rating check (PRC) using the unit's offensive PR (or defensive PR if no offensive PR). The check is at the start of a friendly ENA Movement or Exploitation Phase.
text:
A player may attempt to activate each unit for ENA individually by conducting a proficiency rating check (PRC) using the unit's offensive PR (units without offensive PR use their defensive PR). Conduct the check at the start of a friendly ENA Movement or Exploitation Phase.

**Pass:** Activate the unit.
**Fail:** The unit cannot activate. The failure counts against the number of attempts allowed. Do not fatigue the unit.

title: ENA Restrictions
summary: During an ENA period: units in Strat mode may not move, units may not conduct any construction (and ENA does not count as a GT for ongoing construction), and units may not voluntarily change mode.
text:
During an ENA period:

**a)** Units in Strat mode may not move.
**b)** Units may not conduct any construction. Do not count an ENA period as a GT for ongoing construction.

**Exception:** Units may attempt hasty bridge demolition (17.3.2b).

**c)** Units may not voluntarily change mode.

title: ENA & Fuel
summary: Mech units use the fuel state of their formation in the immediately preceding night GT (16.4.3).
text: Mech units use the fuel state of their formation in the immediately preceding night GT (16.4.3).

title: ENA Sequence of Play
summary: Extended night activity occurs in the following order. There is no Mode Determination Phase or Administration Phase during ENA periods.
text:
Extended night activity occurs in the following order.

**Important:** There is no Mode Determination Phase or Administration Phase during ENA periods.

title: ENA First Player Turn
summary: The ENA First Player Turn consists of: First Player Movement Phase, Second Player Exploit Phase (full MA), First Player Combat Phase, and First Player Exploit Phase (full MA). The first player may use their Exploit Phase either here or in the Second Player Turn, but not both.
text:
**1)** First Player Movement Phase.
**2)** Second Player Exploit Phase (full MA). The second player can choose to use this Exploit Phase or the Second Player Exploit Phase in the Second Player Turn.
**3)** First Player Combat Phase.
**4)** First Player Exploit Phase (full MA). The first player can choose to use this Exploit Phase or the First Player Exploit Phase in the Second Player Turn.

title: ENA Second Player Turn
summary: The ENA Second Player Turn consists of: Second Player Movement Phase, First Player Exploit Phase (full MA) if not already used in the First Player Turn, and Second Player Combat Phase.
text:
**1)** Second Player Movement Phase.
**2)** First Player Exploit Phase (full MA). If the first player conducted an Exploit Phase in the First Player Turn, skip this phase.
**3)** Second Player Combat Phase.

title: ENA Mutual Fatigue Phase
summary: Place all fatigue hits due to any type of ENA activity during the ENA Mutual Fatigue Phase only.
text: Place all fatigue hits due to any type of ENA activity during the ENA Mutual Fatigue Phase only.

title: ENA & Fatigue
summary: A unit incurs one fatigue hit (14.1.0) for each action performed from a specific list during an ENA period, including expending MPs, participating in an offensive GA, defending against a GA using more than one-half its current CS, or an Art unit participating in an offensive or defensive FS mission.
text:
A unit incurs one fatigue hit (14.1.0) for each action performed from the below list during an ENA period:

**a)** If the unit expends any MPs.
**b)** If the unit participates in an offensive GA.
**c)** Defends against a GA using more than one-half its current CS (13.6.1).
**d)** If an Art unit participates in an offensive or defensive FS mission.

**Exception:** Units that are in a MR bonus period are not subject to fatigue during ENA.

**Example:** Two activated German units move and then conduct a TA. Both sides commit 2 Art units to the combat and the defender decides to defend at full strength. At the end of the German player's ENA turn, the 2 German units that moved and attacked would suffer 2 fatigue hits and the 2 German Art units would receive 1 fatigue hit. The Allied defending unit and the two Art units would all receive 1 fatigue hit.

**Important:** See 14.2.0 and 14.3.0 for the effect and recovery of fatigue.

title: Rest Game Turns
summary: Unless scenario rules state otherwise, players must conduct one rest GT each GD. Rest GTs do not impact the ability of a side to conduct ENA.
text:
Unless scenario rules state otherwise, players must conduct one rest GT each GD. Rest GTs do not impact the ability of a side to conduct ENA.

**Example:** The Allied player could conduct an ENA period even though the night GT is the Allied rest GT. Units activated would still suffer the penalties of conducting an ENA period.

title: Allied Rest Turns
summary: All western Allied forces must take a rest turn each night GT unless scenario rules state otherwise. Allied tactical doctrine, especially for CW units, was such that units tended to use this time to reorganize and supply their units.
text:
All western Allied forces must take a rest turn each night GT unless scenario rules state otherwise.

*Designers note: Allied tactical doctrine (especially for CW units) was such that units tended to utilize this time to reorganize and supply their units. With few exceptions, most commanders were either unable or unwilling to attempt the coordination required for a large-scale assault during hours of darkness.*

title: German Rest Turns
summary: Each GD during the Command Phase, the Axis side must secretly designate a rest GT (AM, PM, or Night). Prior to the start of a game, players should agree on whether all armies designate the same rest GT or each army designates its own.
text:
Each GD during the Command Phase, the Axis side must secretly designate a rest GT (AM, PM or Night).

• Prior to the start of a game, players should agree on one of the following options:
**1)** All armies (and all subordinate units) designate the same rest GT.

title: Rest GT Restrictions
summary: During a rest GT, units may not enter PA or Exploit mode, may not move directly from a hex adjacent to an enemy unit to another hex adjacent to that same or different enemy unit, and may not change mode voluntarily.
text:
**Unit Modes:** Units may not enter PA or Exploit mode during a rest GT.

**Important:** Units already in Exploit mode may remain in Exploit mode.

**Movement:** Units may not move directly from a hex adjacent to any enemy unit to another hex adjacent to that same or different enemy unit.

**Exception:** Units marked with an isolated and/or OoS marker, and who move towards their source of GenS are exempt from the above adjacent enemy unit restriction, although normal MHs and MP costs still apply.

title: Rest Turns & Fatigue
summary: A unit incurs one fatigue hit for performing each of certain actions during their rest GT, including moving more than one-half of modified MA, being marked with a TA marker, defending against a GA using more than one-half CS, conducting FS missions over one-half barrage factor, or if marked CR and supporting an offensive or defensive GA.
text:
A unit incurs one fatigue hit for performing each of the following actions during their rest GT:

**a)** Moves more than one-half of its modified MA. Apply the fatigue hit the moment a unit completes its movement in both the Movement and Exploit Phase.

**b)** If marked with a TA marker. Apply the fatigue hit when removing the TA marker (voluntary or involuntary removal).

**Important:** Apply this fatigue hit even if the unit did not conduct a TA.

**c)** Defends against a GA using more than one-half its CS.

**d)** Conducts any type of FS mission using more than one-half of its barrage factor (11.4.1). Apply the fatigue hit when participating in an FS mission.

**e)** If marked CR (5.8.0) and supports an offensive or defensive GA.

**Exception:** Units that are in a MR bonus period (5.4.4d).

title: Exempt Units
summary: Certain units are exempt from rest turn movement restrictions and fatigue penalties, including Mech class units using TPs that remain at least six hexes from an enemy unit, units in a MR Bonus period, MP expended by units placing delay markers, and Art units using in-battery movement.
text:
The following units are exempt from rest turn movement restrictions/fatigue penalties:

**a)** Mech units (including Leg units using TPs) that use Mech road movement and remain at least six hexes from an enemy unit (five intervening hexes).

**b)** Units in a MR Bonus period (5.4.4).

**c)** MP expended by units when placing delay markers does not count towards fatigue (7.13.0).

**d)** Art units using in-battery movement (5.6.1).

title: Unit Characteristics
summary: Discusses unit size, unit steps, movement class, and strength. Players should refer to the UTC when reading this section.
text: This section discusses unit size, unit steps, movement class, and strength. Players should refer to the UTC when reading this section.

title: Unit Size
summary: Unit size affects game functions throughout the rules. Units range in size from Bn.'s (largest) to Z-step units (the smallest).
text: Unit size affects game functions throughout the rules. Units range in size from Bn.'s (largest) to Z-step units (the smallest).

title: Battalions
summary: A Bn. will have a Bn.-size symbol (two vertical lines) on its unit counter. Three-step hybrid units may not always have the Bn. size symbol.
text:
A Bn. will have a Bn.-size symbol (two vertical lines) on its unit counter.

**Important:** Three-step hybrid units may not always have the Bn. size symbol.

• A Bn. can contain from one to three steps (Co.'s). Treat a one or two-step Bn. as Bn. unless otherwise noted in the rules.

**Example:** Art Bn.'s, RE Bn.'s, CW Recon Bn.'s, and HQ units.

• A three-step hybrid unit is always a Bn sized unit.

**Important:** There are Art units designated as Co. and Z-step units. Unless specifically stated in scenario rules, all artillery units (except Z-step units) consist of one step.

title: Companies
summary: A Co. sized unit has a white stripe across the counter behind the unit type box or AFV silhouette and may have a Co. symbol (a single vertical line).
text:
A Co. sized unit has a white stripe across the counter behind the unit type box or AFV silhouette (icon) and may have a Co. symbol (a single vertical line).

• A Co. sized unit can contain one or two steps (Co.'s). A two-step Co. has a second unit type box or AFV icon superimposed over its unit type box/AFV icon.

**Important:** A two-step hybrid Co. unit is still just a single Co. sized unit for all purposes, even though it represents two Co.'s. It is not a Bn.

title: Z-Step Units
summary: A Z-step unit does not have a unit size symbol or step dot; instead it will have a white or red circle behind the unit type box or icon.
text:
A Z-step unit does not have a unit size symbol or step dot, instead it will have a white or red circle behind the unit type box or icon.

• A Co. unit that possesses a Z-step will have a Z-step unit of the same type printed on the reverse of the unit counter. This applies to both Co. BU and Co. unit counters.

• A Co. unit possessing a Z-step may breakdown into two Z-step units (18.8.0).

• If a Co. unit incurs a step loss and the Co. possesses a Z-step, flip the counter over so that its Z-step side shows.

• Regardless of other units in a hex, no more than two Z-step units may stack in one hex (6.1.0).

**When conducting FS missions, Z-step units:**

**a)** Count as one-half a step for unit density (round up) (11.5.4).

**b)** Cannot observe for FS missions (11.2.1).

**c)** Cannot fulfill numerical hits if stacked with a Co. or Bn. sized unit.

**d)** Are eliminated if not stacked with a unit of at least Co. size if subject to any combat result, which requires any type of step loss to the defending hex.

**e)** If a Z-step unit with a defensive factor of zero is the sole unit in a hex, and is the subject of a GA, use the right most column on the CRT and apply shifts normally.

**When participating in a GA:**

**a)** Z-step units may only participate in an offensive GA if stacked with a unit of at least Co. size and that unit is participating in the same GA.

**b)** Z-step units may never be the lead PR or armor unit when conducting an offensive GA if there are any Co. or Bn. sized units participating in the GA (13.8.1 & 13.8.4).

**Exception:** A Pz V, Pz VI or Pz VIb Z-step unit may be the lead PR and/or armor unit. This applies when attacking or defending.

**c)** Z-step units that are the only units in a defending hex during a GA, may be the lead PR and/or lead armor unit.

**d)** Each step loss taken by a mandatory GA step loss result also eliminates one Z-step unit of that side (if any are present). This loss does not satisfy any step loss obligation (13.10.3).

**e)** If after resolving all step losses in a GA, there are no surviving friendly Co. or Bn. sized units in a hex, eliminate all surviving Z-step units in the hex that participated in that GA.

**f)** Eliminate all Z-step units not stacked with a unit of at least Co. size if subject to any GA combat result which requires any type of step loss to the defending hex.

**Exception:** During a GA resolution, if a Co. suffers a step loss that reduces it to a Z-step, do not eliminate that newly created Z-step unit from either of the above two rules.

title: Hybrid Units
summary: Hybrid units represent one or two Co.'s of AFV and one Co. of Mot Inf, Arm Inf, or Arm Recon, and are Mech class units.
text: Hybrid units are units that represent one or two Co.'s of AFV and one Co. of Mot Inf, Arm Inf, or Arm Recon. They are Mech class units. See UTC and 22.6.0.

title: Unit Steps
summary: The number of steps a unit currently possesses is equal to the number of step dots on the unit's counter, with each step representing a Co.
text:
The number of steps a unit currently possesses is equal to the number of step dots on the unit's counter. Each step represents a Co.

• The back of a two or three-step unit depicts the unit after it has suffered one step loss. A color band across the counter's combat and movement values indicates the unit has suffered one step loss.

• The back of a one-step unit may:
**a)** Display a Z-step unit (4.1.3); or,
**b)** Display a unit symbol only.

**Important:** See 22.6.0 to determine hybrid step losses and replacements.

title: Step Losses
summary: When resolving a FS mission or GA results, a single unit cannot suffer additional step losses until all affected units in the hex have suffered an equal number of step losses.
text:
When resolving a FS mission or GA results, a single unit cannot suffer additional step losses until all affected units in the hex have suffered an equal number of step losses.

• Reduce the number of steps in a unit by one for each step loss suffered.

**Important:** A Bn. or two-step Co. unit size remains a Bn. or Co. sized unit regardless of the number of step losses it has sustained. See 4.1.3 for the effect on Co.'s. that have a Z-step on their reverse side.

• Eliminate units when they have lost their last step (remove them from the map).

**Exception:** Co. units that have a Z-step unit on the reverse side of the counter (4.1.3).

• Eliminated units may be eligible for resurrection (22.5.2).

title: Maximum Step Loss
summary: A unit may not suffer more than one step loss in a single GA or FS mission unless it is specifically stated that Max step loss does not apply.
text: A unit may not suffer more than one step loss in a single GA or FS mission unless it is specifically stated that Max step loss does not apply. See special stacking (6.2.0).

title: Taking Step Losses
summary: Mark unit step losses using specific procedures; a unit's PR, armor rating, AT rating and MA are unaffected by losses.
text:
Mark unit step losses using the below procedures:

**Important:** A unit's PR, armor rating, AT rating and MA are unaffected by losses.

**Z-step Units:** Eliminate the unit if it suffers any type of loss (4.1.3).

**One-Step Units:** Eliminate the unit if it suffers one step loss.

**Exception:** If the unit has a Z-step unit on its reverse side, flip the counter so that the Z-step side shows.

**Two-Step Units:**

**First Step Loss:** Flip the counter over so that its reduced side shows. The combat factors printed on the reduced side of the counter are its CS.

**Second Step Loss:** Eliminate the unit when it suffers its second step loss.

**Three Step Units:**

**First Step Loss:** Flip the counter over so that its reduced side shows. The combat factors printed on the reduced side of the counter are its CS.

**Second Step Loss:** The counter continues to have its reduced side showing. Place an ace of spade marker under the unit. Calculate the unit's CS by halving the combat factors shown on its reduced side.

**Example:** If a three-step Bn. with a combat strength of **7-8** takes a step loss, flip it over so that its reduced side is up. The Bn. now has a CS of **5-6**. If the unit suffers a second step loss, place a hit marker under the unit. The Bn.'s CS would now be **3-3** (5÷2 = 2.5 and 6÷2 = 3).

**Third Step Loss:** Eliminate the unit when it suffers its third step loss.

• Players place eliminated units in the corresponding Eliminated Units Box.

**a)** Players must keep units eliminated while isolated separate from other eliminated units (22.5.0).

**b)** Players cannot resurrect eliminated HQ on the same GD of elimination. Place eliminated HQ in the next GD Box on the GTRT. Place them in the Eliminated Units Box at the beginning of that GD.

**Important:** See 22.6.0 to determine hybrid step losses and replacements.

title: Unit Movement Class
summary: A unit's movement class determines the number of MP expended based upon the terrain entered or crossed, and can affect other unit capabilities throughout the rules.
text: A unit's movement class determines the number of MP expended based upon the terrain entered or crossed by the unit as it moves (7.0 & TEC) and can affect other unit capabilities throughout the rules. Each unit is one of three movement classes:

title: Leg Class Units
summary: Leg class units represent units that do not have enough inherent wheeled or tracked vehicles to transport all assigned equipment and personnel.
text:
Leg class units represent units that do not have enough inherent wheeled or tracked vehicles to transport all assigned equipment and personnel.

**Important:** Leg class units are always personnel type units.

title: Mech Class Units
summary: Mech class units represent units that have enough inherent vehicles to transport all personnel or consist primarily of wheeled/tracked vehicles.
text:
Mech class units represent units that:

**a)** Have enough inherent vehicles to transport all personnel; or,

**Example:** Motorized, armored and motorcycle Inf units.

**b)** Consist primarily of wheeled/tracked vehicles (including horse-drawn units).

**Example:** HQs, pure AFV units, towed/SPA units and armored Recon units.

**c)** All HQ are Mech class units unless noted otherwise in scenario rules.

**Important:** Mech class units can be either personnel or vehicular type units. See Unit Type Chart (UTC).

• Leg class units can temporarily become Mech class units using TP (7.12.3a) or Allied inherent transport (7.12.3b).

• Mech class units can dismount (leave their vehicles) and temporarily become Leg class units (7.12.1).

**Important:** Ldr units may switch from Mech class to Leg class units and may use Mech road and non-road movement in the same Exploit or Movement Phase (23.1.0).

title: Static Class Units
summary: Static Art, AT and Flak units are units that have a printed MA of zero; they may have a 'T' printed on the counter or have hollow wheels under the unit type box.
text:
Static Art, AT and Flak units are units that have a printed MA of zero. They may have a **"T"** printed on the counter or have wheels under the unit type box that are hollow (i.e. not blackened in); these hollow wheels show that such units lack organic transport.

• Static units may only move if transported by TP from their superior army HQ's allocation of motorization TPs.

• Static units mount and dismount using the procedures in 7.12.0.

• Static Art units that do not depict a type of motorization symbol (hollow wheels), or only have a unit type symbol (and a unit ID) may not move after a player places them on the map.

• Static Art, AT and Flak units under transport cannot conduct FS mission or offensive GAs. They defend with a strength of 4 and do not possess Flak or AT capabilities.

title: Unit Proficiency Ratings
summary: All units have a PR used for proficiency checks during FS missions and GAs; most units have both an offensive and a defensive PR.
text:
All units have a PR. See the UTC for the location of these ratings on the counters.

• Most units have both, an offensive and a defensive PR.

• There are units that have a defensive PR but not an offensive PR. These units may not participate in an offensive GA if they are the only unit in a hex. They may conduct an offensive GA if stacked with a non-z-step unit that possesses attack proficiency. They may not function as the lead PR unit.

**Exception:** Ranged Art units (those units with a printed range greater than zero) may never participate in an offensive GA.

title: Unit Proficiency Checks
summary: To make a PRC, roll 1d10 and apply any applicable modifiers; if the result equals or exceeds the unit's PR value it fails, if less it passes.
text:
Use the below procedure when making a PRC.

• Roll 1d10, apply any applicable modifiers.

**a)** If the result is equal to or greater than the PR value of the unit, it has failed its PRC.

**b)** If the result is less than the PR value of the unit, it has passed its PRC.

**Important:** If stacked with a Ldr, apply a –1 DRM.

• Unless stated otherwise, use the offensive PR when the unit is on the active side and the defensive PR when the unit is on the inactive side.

• In phases where both sides are active (Command Phase) use the PR rating listed in the specific rule.

title: Ldr Proficiency Checks
summary: Ldr PRCs use the same procedure as unit PRCs, using the Ldr's activation number as its PR.
text: Ldr PRCs use the same procedure as unit PRCs. Use the Ldr's activation number as its PR.

title: BU Co. Proficiency Ratings
summary: Unless stated otherwise in scenario rules, the standard PR ratings (offensive PR/defensive PR) for BU Co.'s are defined by nationality and time period.
text:
Unless stated otherwise in scenario rules, the standard PR ratings (offensive PR/defensive PR) for BU Co.'s are as follows:

**Prior to 1 September 1944**
**German 1-2-6 Leg Infantry Co.:** 4/5
**Other German Leg Co.:** 5/6
**German Mech Co.:** 6/8
**All Allied Co.:** 5/6

**Beginning 1 September 1944**
**German 1-2-6 Leg Infantry Co.:** 4/5
**Other German Leg Co.:** 5/6
**German Mech Co.:** 6/8
**Allied Leg Co.:** 6/6
**Allied Mech Co.:** 6/7

**Important:** Consult scenario rules to determine BU Co. PR for other nationalities.

• A BU Co. can only recombine (18.7.0) back into a unit with the same or lower PR rating.

title: Green Divisions
summary: Historically, formations new to combat did not initially perform well during their introduction; scenario rules will outline which formations are green and any additional special effects.
text:
Historically, formations new to combat did not initially perform well during their introduction to combat. Scenario rules will outline which formations are green and any additional special affects this may incur.

**Important:** This rule does not affect independent corps/army asset units (including Arm CvGps).

• There are three levels of training in the game. From worse to best they are; Raw, Green, and Veteran (Vet).

**GA Effects:**

**Raw:** If any unit in a raw status participates in a GA:
**a)** If part of the attacking force, shift two columns to the left.
**b)** If part of the defending force, shift one column to the right.
**c)** Vet units attached to a raw formation suffer the same GA penalties as the raw units even if no assigned raw units participate in the GA.

**Green:** If any unit in a green status participates in a GA:
**a)** If part of the attacking force, shift one column to the left.
**b)** If part of the defending force, shift one column to the right.
**c)** Vet units attached to a green formation suffer the same GA penalties as the green units even if no assigned green units participate in the GA.

**Command Restrictions:**
**a)** Players may not attach raw and green units to a formation that is itself, in a green or raw status.
**b)** Players may attach raw and green units to a Vet formation.

**Important:** When raw and green units participate in a GA while attached to a Vet formation, that GA suffers the unfavorable column shift(s).

**c)** Raw and green US armored formations may not operate as a divisional formation until at least two combat commands (CC) subordinate to the formation have gained Vet status. Each CC (can include division support units) may operate as an independent BG. Players may attach the CC to a Vet formation, as above.

title: Green Division Checks
summary: A raw or green formation can increase its status when two subordinate Rgts achieve a higher level of experience (raw to green or green to Vet).
text:
A raw or green formation can increase its status when two subordinate Rgts achieve a higher level of experience (raw to green or green to Vet).

• A Rgt may change its status when at least two BN.'s subordinate to the same Rgt contribute RIBs to a successful offensive GA.

**Important:** The offensive GA is successful if the defending units suffered more total hits (mandatory and discretionary) than the attacking units.

• Immediately after the completion of a successful offensive GA, roll 1d10 and if the result is greater than 3, the Rgt gains one level of experience. If the formation has an active divisional Ldr, apply a +1 DRM (the Ldr is on the map).

• Each Rgt can conduct this check after each successful GA.

• If one assigned Rgt of a division makes a successful check, the player may not make any further checks for that division during the current GD.

**Important:** Only one Rgt in each formation can increase its experience by one level per GD.

• Once two Rgts gain a level of experience, the entire formation moves up one level.

title: Special Units & Formations
summary: This section covers special unit types and formations with unique rules, including German Generic Pz Bn.'s and other specialized units.
text: This section covers special unit types and formations with unique rules.

title: German Generic Pz Bn.'s
summary: The Axis side may have generic German Pz Bn. counters, each type-specific, representing one type of panzer that can be utilized to form a generic Pz Bn.
text:
The Axis side may have generic German Pz Bn. counters. Each Bn. is type specific, representing one type of panzer. To utilize a generic Pz Bn.:

• The Axis player may, during a friendly Mode Determination Phase, remove from the map three steps of pure armor that are all the same type as the Pz Bn. The steps must come from the same formation, be in command and GenS.

• He then places a generic Pz Bn. (of the same type as the removed steps) on or adjacent to the formation HQ.

• The generic Pz Bn. functions with the combined strength of all three Pz Co.'s and the PR ratings of any one Co. it replaced (player's choice). Treat the Bn. as a three-step Bn.

• When a generic Pz Bn. suffers a step loss, one of the off-map Pz Co.'s must lose a step (it could be flipped to its z-step side). Two z-step Pz units are equal to one Co., if the Bn. suffers a second step loss (reducing a second Co. to a Z-step unit), the Axis player must recombine the two z-step units into one full strength Co. These off-map Z-step units are not subject to the Z-step loss rules in 4.1.3.

• There is no requirement for the Axis player to reveal the actual strength of a generic Pz Bn. to the Allied side, until the Bn. participates in a GA (offensively or defensively).

• Axis players should keep track of the units assigned to the Bn. on paper or in a makeshift holding box.

• The Axis player may, during a friendly Mode Determination Phase, remove from the map the generic Pz Bn. and replace it with any surviving steps of pure armor in the holding box.

title: Flak Regiments
summary: Unless scenario rules dictate otherwise, specific procedures apply when Luftwaffe Flak units/formations are present.
text:
Unless scenario rules dictate otherwise, follow the below procedures when *Luftwaffe* Flak units/formations are present.

**Important:** Luftwaffe Flak Co.'s do not block Mech road movement unless the Flak Co. is a static unit being transported by TP or if they are in Strat mode.

• *Luftwaffe* Flak Co.'s:
**a)** Are in command and GenS if they can trace a GenS path to any friendly HQ.
**b)** Are not required to be attached or assigned to a superior HQ.
**c)** Do not require fuel (they are always in normal fuel).
**d)** Do not incur the multi-formation penalty when stacked units from other formations.
**e)** Have a limited ability to conduct FS missions (11.4.1b).

**Designer's Note:** The Luftwaffe Flak units were normally under the administration and command of the Reich Defense Command, so command of these units should not be a problem. Luftwaffe Flak formations contain a variable number of Co.'s that consist of motorized 88mm Flak guns (20 and 37mm guns have been abstracted into the units).

title: Rear Echelon Units
summary: Rear Echelon (RE) units have a white circle or box behind their unit size symbol; they cannot breakdown, receive RePs, or resurrect if eliminated.
text:
Rear Echelon (RE) units have a white circle or box behind their unit size symbol. A game may also have additional RE units (not marked). Consult the game's UTC. RE units:
**a)** Cannot breakdown.
**b)** Cannot receive RePs.
**c)** Cannot resurrect if eliminated.
**d)** Do not count as step losses for recycling purposes (22.2.0).
**e)** Have special rules for transferring steps (22.5.1b).
**f)** May provide steps to assist in resurrecting eliminated units (22.5.2c).

title: German Nebelwerfer & Artillery Bdes/Rgts
summary: Nebelwerfer and all Art Bdes/Rgts are army/corps artillery assets without a separate HQ; players may not attach units of these Bdes/Rgts to a formation.
text:
*Nebelwerfer* and all Art Bdes/Rgts are army/corps artillery assets (they do not have a separate HQ). Players may not attach units of these Bdes/Rgts to a formation.

**Important:** Do not treat these Bdes/Rgts as formations. Treat each unit as a corps/army asset unit (9.0).

title: Kangaroos
summary: Games may include Kangaroo transport units that can transport one personnel Leg class Bn. or Co., with all mounting/dismounting rules in effect with some exceptions.
text:
Games may include Kangaroo transport units.

• Each Kangaroo unit may transport one personnel Leg class Bn. or Co.

• All rules pertaining to mounting and dismounting (7.12.0) are in effect with the following exceptions:
**a)** Use of Kangaroos does not reduce the Allied inherent transport capability (7.12.3b).
**b)** Units mounted in Kangaroos are personnel type Mech class units.
**c)** Units mounted in Kangaroos may participate in GAs while mounted.
**d)** They provide the mixed AFV DRM when defending against a FS mission.

title: Commandos
summary: CDOs have special capabilities including the ability to attempt to ignore MHs due to adjacent enemy units, with specific restrictions on their use as lead PR units in GAs.
text:
CDOs have the following capabilities and restrictions:

• CDOs may attempt to ignore MHs due to the presence of adjacent enemy units (7.7.0).
**a)** When encountering a hex subject to MH, roll 1d10 and if the result is a 0–4, they may ignore the MH in that hex.
**b)** They may make this attempt in every hex they enter which requires a MH.

**Exception:** If motorized using TP or Allied inherent transport, a CDO may not use the above benefit.

• Players may not select a CDO unit as the lead PR unit in a GA unless:
**a)** The CDO unit is participating in an offensive GA and the defending hex contains a Fort, a fortified area hex, or is a special objective detailed in scenario rules; or,
**b)** CDO units are the only units participating in a GA (offensively or defensively).

• Players may only attach one CDO to any formation.

• See 13.7.4c for special engineering capabilities.

title: Engineer AFVs
summary: Scenario rules will identify AFV units with special combat engineer capabilities; Eng AFVs are pure AFV units for all purposes and each step is the equivalent of one step of Eng.
text:
Scenario rules will identify those AFV units with special combat engineer capabilities. Eng AFVs have the following capabilities:
**a)** They are pure AFV units for all purposes.
**b)** For GA purposes, each step is the equivalent of one step of Eng.
**c)** They do not possess Eng construction/bridge demolition or ferrying capabilities.

**Important:** Eng AFVs identified as flame tanks have an **"F"** in place of their offensive combat strength. These units may be part of an offensive GA, but do not contribute to the total strength of the attacking force. However, they may provide combat Eng shifts (13.7.4b).

title: CW Recon Bn.'s
summary: A CW Recon Bn. is subject to specific restrictions regarding offensive GAs, stacking as lead PR, and armor/AT lead roles.
text:
A CW Recon Bn. is subject to the following restrictions:

• If alone in a hex, it cannot conduct an offensive GA unless the only unit in the enemy hex is a z-step unit.

• If stacked with other units, it cannot be the lead PR unit.

• If stacked with other units with an armor or AT factor, it cannot be the lead armor/AT unit.

**Important:** In **Wacht** and **Hurtgen**, CW Recon Bn.'s indicate they are three-step units. Treat them as two step Bn.'s. Their combat strengths remain the same.

title: Unit Modes
summary: The mode a player chooses for each unit represents the unit's tactical configuration during a GT; there are five modes suited for different activities combining movement and combat functions.
text:
The mode a player chooses for each unit represents the unit's tactical configuration during a GT. Each mode is suited for different activities; one mode might combine movement and combat functions while another sacrifices mobility for greater combat power.

• Players may only voluntarily change unit modes during a friendly Mode Determination Phase.

**Exceptions:** Leaving MR mode (5.4.3) or when adjusting attacker status (12.0).

• Combat results and other circumstances may cause a unit to involuntarily change its mode.

**Example:** A unit in PA mode that retreats due to a FS mission or GA removes its PA marker and reverts to Tac mode.

• Units can only be in one mode at a time.

• There is no requirement for a unit to stay in the same mode from turn to turn.

• All units in the game will be in one of five modes:
**Tactical Mode** (5.1.0)
**Prepared Assault** Mode (5.2.0)
**Exploit Mode** (5.3.0)
**Maneuver Reserve** Mode (5.4.0)
**Strategic Mode** (5.5.0)

title: Tactical (Tac) Mode
summary: Tac mode is the most flexible in terms of maneuver and combat and is the default setting for a unit without a mode marker.
text:
Tac mode is the most flexible in terms of maneuver and combat. Tac mode is the default setting for a unit without a mode marker.

**Important:** Do not confuse unit mode with movement type. Units in Tac mode can move using Mech road movement or non-road movement. Units in Exploit Mode can use Mech road movement or non-road movement. Note: Units in Strat mode must only use Mech road movement.

title: Movement
summary: See 7.0 for Tac mode movement procedures.
text:
See 7.0 for Tac mode movement procedures.

**Important:** Do not confuse tactical mode with tactical assault designation.

title: Tactical Assault (TA) Designation
summary: Units in Tac mode may be marked with a tactical assault (TA) marker during the Attack Designation Segment of the Combat Phase; such units are designated to conduct tactical assault.
text:
Units in Tac mode may be marked with a tactical assault (TA) marker during the Attack Designation Segment of the Combat Phase (10.2.0).

**Important:** Units transported by TP or Allied inherent transport cannot be attack designated (7.12.3).

• Placement of a TA marker on a unit and/or stack of units designates them as eligible to conduct tactical assault.

• Units marked with a TA marker:
**a)** Must participate in a GA if adjacent to an enemy unit at the start of the GA Segment.
**b)** Receive no benefit for terrain, population features or FWs/fort/fortified areas when the target of an FS mission.
**c)** Are always observable by adjacent units (even if the units marked TA are in covering terrain).
**d)** May always observe enemy units in adjacent hexes, regardless of the terrain or features the enemy units are in.

• Units marked with a TA marker cannot:
**a)** Combine with units in other hexes to conduct a multi-hex GA (13.4.0).
**b)** Advance more than one hex after GA (13.12.0).
**c)** Spot for a heavy FS mission (11.2.4).

• A player removes TA markers on friendly units:
**a)** During the Attacker Adjustment Segment (12.0). This removal may be voluntary or mandatory.
**b)** After advancing due to a FS mission (11.7.0).
**c)** After a retreat due to an FS mission (11.6.2).
**d)** After a unit conducts an offensive GA (13.11.0).

title: Other Functions
summary: A unit must be in Tac mode to perform several key functions including breaking down into component Co.'s, being designated combat reserve, reducing fatigue level, and conducting ferry operations.
text:
A unit must be in Tac mode to:
**a)** Breakdown into component Co.'s or recombine into their parent unit (18.0).
**b)** Be designated combat reserve (5.8.0).
**c)** Reduce fatigue level (14.3.0).
**d)** Conduct ferry operations (Eng units only) (17.2.0).

**Exception:** Eng units in PA mode can ferry leg units that are also in PA mode (17.2.1).

**e)** Perform any kind of construction tasks (17.3.0).
**f)** Receive replacements (22.5.0).

**Important:** Units in MR mode can also receive replacements (5.4.0).

**g)** Enter the map as reinforcements.

**Exception:** They may also enter in Strat mode (21.0).

**Important:** Scenario rules may allow units to enter as reinforcements in other modes.

title: Prepared Assault Mode
summary: Prepared assault (PA) mode maximizes attack and advance after combat capabilities; units enter PA mode during a friendly Mode Determination Phase and are marked with a PA marker.
text:
Prepared assault (PA) mode maximizes attack and advance after combat capabilities.

• Units enter PA mode during a friendly Mode Determination Phase.

• Mark such units with a PA marker.

title: PA Mode Restrictions
summary: Units may not enter PA mode during a rest GT; specific unit types including HQs, artillery, overstacked units, OoS units, fatigued units, OoC units, and others may not enter PA mode.
text:
**Important:** Units may not enter PA mode during a rest GT.

The following units may not enter PA mode:
**a)** HQs
**b)** Artillery units.
**c)** Overstacked units (6.7.0).
**d)** Out of supply (OoS) units (15.6.0).
**e)** Fatigue-2 units (14.2.2).
**f)** Out of command (OoC) units (9.8.0).
**g)** Units that could not conduct PA movement to reach a hex that is adjacent to an enemy unit.
**h)** Units transported by TPs or Allied inherent transport (7.12.3a & b).

**Important:** Players may not place units in PA mode where such placement would force the player to conduct more GAs than allowed by 16.3.3.

title: PA Mode & No/Low Fuel
summary: If a Mech unit's formation is in a low/no fuel state, the formation must meet one of two requirements for Mech units to enter PA mode.
text:
If a Mech unit's formation is in a low/no fuel state, the formation must meet one of the below requirements for Mech units to enter PA mode.

**a)** If a player determined the formation's fuel value using the option 1 procedure in 16.4.5b, and the formation's fuel value is a minimum of six MP, then any number of that formation's units may enter PA mode; or,

**b)** If the player determined the formation's fuel value using the option 2 procedure in 16.4.5b, and it is less than six, then the owning player can only place the number of units into PA mode that is less than or equal to result of the fuel value DR. No other units subordinate to the formation may enter PA mode.

**Important:** Fuel levels do not affect a Leg class unit's ability to enter or move in PA mode.

title: PA Movement
summary: Units in PA mode move using PA movement, which does not use MP; instead the distance is determined in the number of hexes moved.
text:
Units in PA mode move using PA movement. This type of movement does not use MP. PA movement is determined in the number of hexes moved.

• A unit using PA movement:
**a)** Cannot enter or cross prohibited terrain that it could not move into or across when using tactical movement.

**Important:** Eng units in PA mode may ferry Leg class units also marked PA across streams and rivers (17.2.0).

**b)** Must halt movement as soon as it moves adjacent to an enemy unit. This does not prohibit a unit from beginning PA movement adjacent to one enemy unit and then moving adjacent to another enemy unit, or another hex adjacent to the same enemy unit.

**Exception:** 7.9.0.

• PA movement limits are as follows:
**Leg class units:** May move one hex.

**Important:** Leg class CDO units may move two hexes, except during any type of mud ground condition or night GTs.

**Mech class units:** May move up to two hexes during AM and PM GTs. Mech class units may only move one hex if:
**a)** It is a night GT.
**b)** If any type of mud ground condition is in effect unless the unit moves entirely along primary or secondary roads.
**c)** If the unit's formation HQ is in a low or no fuel state.

**Important:** A Mech unit must halt PA movement when entering a wood, forest, marsh, soft ground, swamp, bocage, hedgerow, RR embankment or constricted terrain hex if it is not entering the hex along a road (of any type).

title: Combat
summary: Units in PA mode must participate in a GA if adjacent to an enemy unit at the GA Segment start, receive no terrain benefit as FS mission targets, and gain additional advance and observation benefits.
text:
Units in PA mode:

• Must participate in a GA if adjacent to an enemy unit at the start of the GA Segment.

• If the target of an FS mission, they do not receive any benefit for terrain, population features or FWs/fort/fortified areas.

**Important:** See 8.2.1 & 8.2.2 for PA mode and observation.

• May always observe enemy units in adjacent hexes regardless of the terrain or features the enemy units are in.

• May combine with units in other hexes that are in PA mode to conduct a multi-hex offensive GA (13.4.0).

• May advance more than one hex after GA (13.12.3).

• Receive a one-column shift in their favor when conducting a GA (13.7.3).

• If a Bn. sized unit, may spot for a heavy FS mission (11.2.4).

title: Removing PA Markers
summary: A player removes PA markers on friendly units after a retreat due to an FS mission, during the Attacker Adjustment Segment, or after conducting a GA.
text:
A player removes PA markers on friendly units:
**a)** After a retreat due to an FS mission (11.6.3).
**b)** During the Attacker Adjustment Segment (12.0). This removal may be voluntary or mandatory.
**c)** After conducting a GA.

title: Exploitation Mode
summary: Exploit mode allows units to move during the Exploit Phase giving them a chance to react to offensive or defensive opportunities outside the normal Movement Phase.
text:
Exploit mode allows units to move during the Exploit Phase giving them a chance to react to offensive or defensive opportunities outside the normal Movement Phase.

• Units may enter Exploit mode:
**a)** During a non-rest GT (3.5.3) friendly Mode Determination Phase; or,
**b)** During a friendly Exploit Phase if the units are in Maneuver Reserve (MR).

• Units entering Exploit mode:
**a)** Cannot be fatigue–1 or Fatigue-2; and,
**b)** Cannot be adjacent to an enemy unit; and,
**c)** Must be in GenS and in command; and,
**d)** Cannot be in a no fuel state.
**e)** Must be a Mech class unit or a Leg class unit motorized using TP or Allied inherent transport.

**Important:** If fuel points are not allocated to a unit's formation during the Logistics Phase, the unit must revert to Tac mode during that Logistics Phase.

• Mark units entering Exploit mode with an Exploit marker.

• Units in Exploit mode may only move during a friendly Exploit Phase. They may not voluntarily move in any other phase.

• Units do not enter or exit Exploit mode individually; they must do so as part of a formation or Rgt.

**a)** To enter Exploit mode, all Mech class units subordinate to a formation or Rgt (this includes attached units) must enter Exploit mode.
**b)** Mech class divisional asset units (26.0) can enter Exploit mode along with a Rgt subordinate to the same formation. There is no limit to the number of divisional asset units that may enter Exploit mode with one Rgt.

**Example:** A division could have all units of one of its Rgts enter Exploit mode. However, it could not have part of that Rgt enter Exploit while other units of the same Rgt do not.

title: Recon Units & Exploit
summary: Individual Mech class Recon units of any size may enter Exploit mode without having to place the whole formation/Rgt into Exploit mode.
text:
Individual Mech class Recon units of any size (UTC) may enter Exploit mode without having to place the whole formation/Rgt into Exploit mode.

• All other rules regarding entering Exploit mode are in effect.

• Recon units in Exploit mode may not stack with units not in Exploit mode at any time.

• If at the end of any phase, a Recon unit is in a stack with a unit not in Exploit mode, the owning player must immediately remove the Exploit marker from the Recon unit.

• A Recon unit may stack with any unit in Exploit mode allowed by special stacking (6.2.0).

title: Exiting Exploit Mode
summary: The owning player may voluntarily remove Exploit markers during the Mode Determination Phase; there are specific phases in which the owning player must remove an Exploit marker.
text:
The owning player may voluntarily remove Exploit markers during the Mode Determination Phase.

• The owning player must remove an Exploit marker from a unit in the phases below:
**a)** The Administration Phase if the unit is OoS.
**b)** The Exploit Phase if:
**1)** The unit is adjacent to an enemy unit in PA mode (5.3.4a).
**2)** The unit retreats due to a result of an FS mission (5.3.4c).
**3)** The unit conducts an unsuccessful overrun (5.3.5b).
**c)** The Combat Phase if forced to involuntarily remove its Exploit marker (5.3.5c).

title: Reentering Exploit Mode
summary: Individual units forced to exit Exploit mode involuntarily may re-enter Exploit mode during a subsequent non-rest GT Mode Determination Phase under specific conditions.
text:
Individual units forced to exit Exploit mode involuntarily may re-enter Exploit mode during a subsequent non-rest GT (3.5.3) Mode Determination Phase if:
**a)** At least one unit of their formation or Rgt is still in Exploit mode; and,
**b)** The unit is not adjacent to an enemy unit; and,
**c)** The unit is in GenS and command.

title: Exploit Movement
summary: Each side has two Exploitation Phases in each GT; units in Exploit mode may move in a specific order and may move up to one half their modified MA in both friendly Exploit Phases.
text:
Each side has two Exploitation Phases in each GT.

• Units in Exploit mode may move in the following order:
**a)** Units using Mech road movement.
**b)** Units using non-road movement.

• During AM and PM GTs units in Exploit mode may move up to one half (round up) their modified MA in both friendly Exploit Phases.

• During Night GTs and ENA periods, each side may use only one of their two friendly Exploit Phases. Units moving in that one Exploit Phase may move up to their full modified MA.

**Important:** During a night GT or ENA period, the owning player must choose one of the two friendly Exploit Phases during that GT/ENA period to move all his units in Exploit mode.

**Important:** If marked OhS, a unit in Exploit mode is immediately marked OoS if it expends any MP during an Exploit Phase.

title: Exploit & Enemy Units
summary: Units in Exploit mode do not pay additional MPs to move adjacent to enemy units if the hex entered contains MCT; a unit beginning an Exploit Phase adjacent to an enemy unit in PA mode must retreat.
text:
• Units in Exploit mode do not pay additional MPs to move adjacent to enemy units if the hex entered contains MCT.

• A unit in Exploit mode that begins an Exploit Phase adjacent to an enemy unit in PA mode must retreat.

**Important:** The presence of an adjacent enemy unit with a TA marker does not force a unit to exit Exploit mode.

**a)** The unit in Exploit mode may retreat up to the maximum retreat distance allowed by 13.11.1.
**b)** After completion of this retreat, the unit reverts to Tac mode (remove the Exploit marker).
**c)** The enemy player may advance the units in PA mode one hex.
**d)** After completing the advance, the owning player removes the PA marker; and,
**1)** Replaces the PA marker with a TA marker if the unit is adjacent to an enemy unit and the owning player wishes to conduct a tactical assault; or,
**2)** If the advancing unit is not adjacent to an enemy unit or the owning player does not wish to conduct a tactical assault, he then simply removes the PA marker.

title: Exploit & Overrun
summary: Overruns combine Exploit movement and combat allowing units in Exploit mode to displace or destroy enemy forces while on the move; only Mech class units (not Leg class units transported by TP or Allied inherent transport) may conduct an overrun.
text:
Overruns combine Exploit movement and combat allowing units in Exploit mode to displace or destroy enemy forces while on the move.

• Only Mech class units (not Leg class units transported by TP or Allied inherent transport) may conduct an overrun.

**Exception:** Units transported by Kangaroos (4.6.5).

• Only one unit at a time can conduct an overrun (i.e., the player may not conduct an overrun using a stack of units).

**Important:** Treat all units stacked using special stacking (6.2.0) as one unit for this rule.

• The following units cannot conduct an overrun:
**a)** OoS units.
**b)** OoC units.
**c)** Fatigued units (level 1 or level 2).
**d)** Units in a hex without MCT (last hex prior to entering the overrun hex) if that hex is adjacent to more than one enemy occupied hex.

**Important:** During night GTs, treat all OCT as MCT.

• The unit conducting the overrun must be able to enter the overrun hex. The overrunning unit:
**a)** May begin its Exploit movement by declaring and conducting an overrun against an adjacent unit or may move and when entering a hex adjacent to an enemy unit, immediately declare an overrun against the adjacent enemy unit.
**b)** Expends one additional MP to conduct the overrun (this is in addition to the MP cost of entering the overrun hex).
**c)** Expends all normal hex and hexside terrain costs to enter the hex being overrun, including the cost of moving adjacent to an enemy unit (7.8.0).
